projects.title: Planet of the Apes: Last Frontier
projects.summary: Recovered and rebuilt a console Unreal project with missing build instructions and documented the entire process so future engineers can reproduce builds.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Planet of the Apes was delivered to Catalog Operations with zero build instructions. The original developers were no longer available and the console toolchains were not set up.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: I determined the exact Unreal Engine and console SDK versions, rebuilt the editor and console toolchains, disabled online components per policy, updated legal/localization data, and iterated until the game built and ran on target hardware.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Complete build documentation and reproducible builds were produced for archival purposes. Although the title was later cancelled, the archive now contains a runnable process and engineering notes for future maintainers.

projects.title: Star Wars: Assault Team
projects.summary: Updated an older Unity mobile title to run on modern Android devices and Amazon Fire tablets with improved asset pipelines to decrease load times.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Star Wars Assault Team was aging on older Android versions and lacked compatibility for modern devices and Amazon Fire tablets.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Upgraded the project across Unity versions (stepwise), fixed API deprecations, rewrote build scripts and asset-bundling tools to reduce build time and improve load performance.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Faster builds, improved level-load times, and successful deployments to Amazon and other marketplaces. This allowed the project to reach wider international audiences.

projects.title: Toy Story Drop
projects.summary: Transitioned a live freemium game into Disney ownership, improved cross-platform tooling, and implemented gameplay changes to support new monetization models.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: The original developer moved on. Per contract, the title needed to be transferred to Disney and made buildable across platforms.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Worked with the original producer to transfer assets and code, produced functioning builds across platforms, and improved cross-platform tools. Implemented gameplay adjustments for a premium model and removed IAP where required.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: The game launched on additional Android platforms and was licensed to certain PC platforms, extending its reach beyond initial expectations.

projects.title: Inside Out: Thought Bubbles
projects.summary: Updated an older Unity title to modern engine versions, removed IAP, and adjusted UI for newer phone aspect ratios.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Needed to update Unity 5.x project to Unity 2017.x and transition from F2P to premium, while improving UI for newer devices.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Performed staged engine upgrades, removed IAP, adjusted UI/HUD for wider screens (iPhone X), and optimized asset bundle strategy to reduce memory usage.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Improved visuals and cross-platform support enabled the game to run on additional markets without IAP.

projects.title: Disney Dreamlight Valley — Renders
projects.previewSub: THE WALT DISNEY COMPANY · BOOK PUBLISHING
projects.summary: Built tools to batch-place scene assets and render high-resolution house images for a strategy guide book.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Publisher needed clean renders of in-game houses without surrounding environments on tight timelines.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Wrote Unity scripts to place assets, set cameras, and render batches at multiple resolutions. Iterated lighting and composition quickly.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Delivered final print-ready renders in days, enabling rapid iteration with art and publishing teams.

projects.title: Beauty and the Beast: Perfect Match
projects.summary: Upgraded Unity to support iPhone X, fixed shader/asset bundle issues, and adapted HUD for new device aspect ratios.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Game required UI adjustments for iPhone X and experienced shader glitches after engine upgrades.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Implemented an adaptive overlay for the notch, adjusted HUD anchors, and investigated shader bugs (working with Unity where necessary).

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Title supported modern devices with improved UI and performance, and shipped updates on schedule.

projects.title: Disney Mobile Games (Catalog)
projects.summary: Maintained and upgraded multiple legacy Disney mobile titles for broader platform distribution and improved tooling for faster turnarounds.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Catalog of legacy titles required updates to run on modern platforms and to meet security/policy requirements.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Upgraded engines, fixed platform-specific issues, and built tooling to automate repetitive tasks which enabled rapid packaging and deployment.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Multiple titles were re-released on new marketplaces with improved stability and performance.

projects.title: RAW DATA
projects.summary: Room-scale VR combat had no established playbook. Helped build the systems framework that made physical VR combat feel credible, defining what action games in VR could be. One of the first successful room-scale multiplayer titles on the market.

expanded.name: OVERVIEW
expanded.description: Joined Survios as a gameplay engineer during Raw Data's final year — one of the flagship VR titles of the era. Owned analytics, cross-platform achievements, three major demos, and Raw Data Arcade, all built on a plugin architecture that carried forward into future Survios titles.

expanded.name: ANALYTICS
expanded.description: Designed and shipped a UE4 analytics plugin using a third-party data backend over REST. Aggregated kill/death/session data during play and flushed on level transitions, with local persistence as a fallback for crashes or connectivity loss. The system was reliable enough to be adopted across multiple subsequent Survios titles and directly informed late-stage gameplay tuning.

expanded.name: ACHIEVEMENTS
expanded.description: Implemented cross-platform achievements for Steam, Oculus Home, and PlayStation Trophies. Unified the wildly different platform APIs behind a single local data store — progress tracked locally, reported to the platform on threshold. Unlock thresholds were exposed as data containers in the UE4 Editor so designers could iterate without touching code.

expanded.name: DEMOS
expanded.description: Shipped three standalone demos: an HTC Vive retail demo for Valve deployed to Best Buy and GameStop; an E3 2017 booth build where lines stretched two hours at Survios's largest E3 presence ever; and the demo included on the launch PSVR Demo Disk bundled with every initial PSVR kit.

expanded.name: ARCADE
expanded.description: Led development of Raw Data Arcade — a timed, operator-customizable version of the game built for location-based VR. Designed the flow alongside a game designer, and built an operator dashboard in UMG giving arcade managers control over timers, heroes, unlocks, and levels with no engineering involvement. Shipped to over 1,000 arcades worldwide.

expanded.name: OUTCOME
expanded.description: Raw Data became one of the most successful early VR titles on the market — a headliner in over 1,000 arcades globally, a landmark for room-scale multiplayer, and the technical foundation for Survios's engine and platform work on future titles.

projects.title: X-COM 2
projects.summary: Room-scale VR combat had no established playbook. Helped build the systems framework that made physical VR combat feel credible — defining what action games in VR could be. One of the first successful room-scale multiplayer titles on the market.

expanded.name: OVERVIEW
expanded.description: Skydance was contracted to help ship the console ports of XCOM 2. I contributed UI features, two weapons for the single-player campaign, and owned the disc/digital installation requirements for Xbox One and PlayStation 4.

expanded.name: WEAPONS
expanded.description: Implemented two abilities: Triple-Shot — a Sniper pistol ability firing 3 high-accuracy rounds in quick succession; and Saturation Fire (aka Shredder Fire) — a Grenadier chain-gun ability that unleashes a bullet hailstorm in a cone, damaging every enemy and piece of cover within it. A satisfying one to ship.

expanded.name: CONSOLE INSTALLATION
expanded.description: Owned disc and digital installation requirements for Xbox One and PS4, both of which mandate playability within the first 10% of a download. Solved XCOM 2's procedural generation constraints by marking the tutorial mission and Avenger as priority assets, blocking all other streaming until the full download completed. Also updated Unreal Frontend with current-gen console support — new RPCs and streamlined launcher buttons that meaningfully improved the team's daily workflow.

projects.title: 360 VR VIDEO PLAYER
projects.summary: Prototyped and developed a 360 video player for the GearVR.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Audio and video were a medium many wanted to explore in VR, but there were no established tools or pipelines. I built a 360 video player for Android that supported both monoscopic and stereoscopic videos with binaural audio support. This was used for several early WEVR projects and demos.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Created a flexible video player that allowed for multiple spherical projection formats, adjustable procedural generation for playback mesh, support for multiple spatial audio formats combined with very low-latency response to head movement.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: The video player was used for several early WEVR projects and demos, including the Adult Swim Virtual Brainload experience. It was a valuable tool for exploring the potential of video in VR and helped pave the way for future VR video experiences.

projects.title: THEBLU VR PROTOTYPE
projects.summary: Hacked and protoyped a functional VR experience in Unity for the Oculus DK1. This was a proof of concept that eventually evolved into TheBlu, one of the first VR experiences on the market.

expanded.name: problem
expanded.description: Movement mechanics, low-framerates and general VR best practices were still being discovered.

expanded.name: solution
expanded.description: Experimented with multiple movement mechanics, including Xbox 360 controller input, view-direction control and even keyboard-mouse input.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: Ultimately settled on a static viewpoint with a simple gaze-based interaction system. This was a comfortable and intuitive way to explore the underwater environments we created.

projects.title: USC - MASTERS GAMES PROGRAM
projects.summary: A fun but rigorous program that covered a wide range of game development topics. Several projects across different platforms helped define my approach to game development and prepared me for the industry.

expanded.name: bloom
expanded.description: A 2.5D platformer built in Unity where you play as a seedling named Sprout who wields the powers of nature to take back Earth from the evil machines. A year-long project where I wore many hats including graphics developer, gameplay programmer and even tech-art project management.

expanded.name: streets of india
expanded.description: An experimental 3D mobile game built in Unity for iOS, and modelled after Frogger, where the player must guide their character through the busy and hectic streets of India to disarm and remove bombs set at famous landmarks. It was well received by the class and other students in the program.

expanded.name: software rasterizer
expanded.description: Semester-long graphics project where we coded the rendering pipeline for a software rasterizer from scratch in C++; including matrices, rasterization, z-buffering, texturing, etc.

expanded.name: outcome
expanded.description: The program was an invaluable experience that prepared me for the industry. It provided a strong foundation in game development principles and practices, and allowed me to explore different areas of game development through hands-on projects.
